setting.note: Ninety-year-old Hagar Shipley, proud, unbending and afraid, looks back over her hard life on the Manitoba prairie as she flees her son's house rather than be put in a home. Laurence's Manawaka, drawn from the real prairie town of Neepawa, anchors the memory: the Currie store and the blind marble angel in the cemetery, the Shipley farm of her rough marriage, and the west-coast city where her old age plays out its last defiant days.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Margaret Laurence, first published 1964. Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: The Stone Angel, Margaret Laurence (1964). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author's estate or publisher.

palette.note: The grey stone of the blind marble angel over the family graves, the monument to a pride as cold and unseeing as Hagar's own. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
